remedies.title: Honey & Lemon Tea
instructions: Boil water and let it cool slightly (very hot water destroys honey's enzymes).
Stir in the honey until dissolved.
Add the lemon juice and stir.
Sip slowly while warm, up to three times a day.
remedies.origin: Found in folk traditions on every continent; honey has been used to soothe throats since ancient Egypt.
remedies.disclaimer: Never give honey to infants under one year old (risk of infant botulism).

remedies.title: Fresh Ginger Tea
instructions: Peel and thinly slice the ginger.
Simmer the slices in water for 10 minutes.
Strain into a cup and sweeten with honey if you like.
Drink warm in small sips.
remedies.origin: A staple of Ayurvedic and East Asian home care for settling the stomach.
remedies.disclaimer: If you are pregnant or on blood thinners, check with your doctor before using ginger regularly.

remedies.title: Warm Saltwater Gargle
instructions: Dissolve the salt fully in warm (not hot) water.
Gargle a mouthful for 15–30 seconds, then spit it out. Do not swallow.
Repeat until the cup is finished, up to three times a day.
remedies.origin: One of the oldest recorded home treatments, appearing in traditions from China to the Mediterranean.

remedies.title: Chamomile Bedtime Tea
instructions: Pour hot water over the chamomile.
Cover and steep for 5 minutes, then strain.
Drink about 30 minutes before bed.
remedies.origin: Used as a calming evening drink in Europe and North Africa for centuries.
remedies.disclaimer: Avoid if you are allergic to plants in the daisy family (ragweed, marigold).

remedies.title: Steam Inhalation

ingredients.name: eucalyptus leaves or a drop of eucalyptus oil (optional)

instructions: Fill a bowl with steaming (not boiling) water and place it on a stable table.
Add eucalyptus if using.
Drape a towel over your head, lean over the bowl at a safe distance, close your eyes, and breathe slowly through your nose for 5–10 minutes.
remedies.origin: Common cold-season practice across Europe, Africa and the Americas.
remedies.disclaimer: Keep a safe distance from the water to avoid scalding. Not recommended for young children.

remedies.title: Colloidal Oatmeal Bath
instructions: Grind the oats into a fine powder with a blender.
Sprinkle the powder into a lukewarm bath and stir until the water looks milky.
Soak for 15–20 minutes, then pat the skin dry gently.
remedies.origin: A settler and farmhouse tradition later validated as a skin-soothing ingredient in modern dermatology.

remedies.title: Cold Compress for Headache

ingredients.name: cold water or wrapped ice pack

instructions: Soak the cloth in cold water and wring it out, or wrap an ice pack in the cloth.
Lie down in a quiet, dim room.
Place the compress on your forehead or the back of your neck for 15 minutes.
remedies.origin: A near-universal first response to headaches in household traditions worldwide.
remedies.disclaimer: Never place ice directly on skin. See a doctor for sudden, severe, or unusual headaches.

remedies.title: Peppermint Tea After Meals
instructions: Steep the peppermint in hot water for 5–7 minutes.
Strain and drink warm after a heavy meal.
remedies.origin: Mint tea is the everyday digestive of the Maghreb, poured after almost every meal.
remedies.disclaimer: Peppermint can worsen acid reflux in some people.

remedies.title: Clove for Toothache
instructions: Place a whole clove next to the sore tooth.
Let it soften and release its oils; keep it there for up to 20 minutes.
Repeat as needed while you arrange to see a dentist.
remedies.origin: Clove's numbing oil (eugenol) has been used for dental pain in Indian and Indonesian tradition for centuries.
remedies.disclaimer: This eases pain only — a persistent toothache needs a dentist.

remedies.title: Rice Water for Upset Stomach
instructions: Boil the rice in the water for 15–20 minutes.
Strain off the cloudy liquid and let it cool to warm.
Add a small pinch of salt and sip throughout the day.
Eat the rice separately if you feel up to it.
remedies.origin: A gentle rehydration drink used across Asia and Latin America for stomach upsets.
remedies.disclaimer: Diarrhea lasting more than two days, or any diarrhea in infants, needs medical attention.
